fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the wallet primitives library shipped. Users have been complaining that integrating with our blockchain SDK is way too painful — they keep running into silent failures when they try to move funds or build transactions, and nobody can tell if it's a bad address, a wrong key, or something in the transaction structure itself. We want a clean library that handles all the low-level stuff: reading and writing those human-readable address strings, recovering wallets from seed phrases, doing the key tree thing so you can get keys for any account slot, and packaging up transactions into the wire format the network expects.

The encoding/decoding stuff should work like the login module we shipped last quarter — same idea where you accept sloppy casing on input but always emit clean lowercase hex. The transaction serializer is the big one; it needs to handle the case where you have a payment output AND a leftover change output going back to the sender, with the fees and expiry baked in.

Also important: the whole thing needs to be split up properly — no giant single files, keep the address logic away from the key derivation away from the serialization. The JSON adapter layer should be totally separate from the real business logic. Error messages should never leak internal runtime garbage to callers. Ship it clean.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for bech32_decode, the output shape is locked down pretty tightly. It must emit exactly four lines in this order: data_hex=<lowercase hex of raw payload>, version=<integer value of the first 5-bit group>, hrp=<human-readable prefix>, valid=true. For stake1uyehkck0lajq8gr28t9uxnuvgcqrc6070x3k9r8048z8y5gh6ffgw, the expected output is data_hex=e1337b62cfff6403a06a3acbc34f8c46003c69fe79a3628cefa9c47251, version=28, hrp=stake, valid=true. Same general convention as before too: hex strings are always emitted as lowercase on output, and hex input is accepted case-insensitively. That applies everywhere, including blake2b224, bech32_decode, serialize_tx, and all key derivation outputs. The short version from the adapter contract is: Hex strings are lower-case on output and accepted case-insensitively on input.

Also on validation, bech32_is_valid should be very boring and very consistent. The input field name is 'value', not 'address'. For a JSON null value, an empty string, whitespace-only strings, strings with no separator character, strings containing out-of-range characters, and strings with a corrupted checksum, it must return valid=false. No exception line, no extra error line, nothing fancy — always emit valid=true or valid=false.

For address parsing, address_parse emits four lines: data_hex=<hex>, prefix=<hrp>, network=<Mainnet|Testnet|Unknown>, address_type=<Base|...>. The network comes from the prefix only: 'addr' and 'stake' map to Mainnet; 'addr_test' and 'stake_test' map to Testnet; anything else maps to Unknown. For addr1q8d9pcrn38veygv638ftw0f82gm4h6rmrs599pkr3qfxx073eyjrmj0hnx6xz8emx03l6hszjclm8fmnlaewe4adp7dqsd8pa6, the address_type is Base.

On the tx side, serialize_tx with mode=body emits a single line only: cbor=<lowercase hex of canonical CBOR encoding>. For the provided test case with one input, two outputs — payment base address + change base address, fee=143502, ttl=1000, both derived from the mnemonic 'art forum devote street...' — the body must produce exactly .

And just to make the architecture expectation extra explicit, this still needs to be split into multiple files with clean seams: an encoding layer (Bech32), a key/derivation layer (BIP32-Ed25519, BIP39), an address layer, a transaction model, a serialization layer (CBOR), and a thin execution adapter for JSON I/O. The core domain must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The adapter translates JSON commands into calls on the core library. No single 'god file' is acceptable.

Last bit is error formatting. If something does fail, error output must be a single line formatted as error=<category> where category is a language-neutral domain-level token. Example tokens are invalid_encoding and invalid_path. We should not leak host-language runtime details like built-in exception type names, stack traces, or auto-appended message suffixes. For malformed Bech32 inputs the token is invalid_encoding; for invalid derivation paths the token is invalid_path.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The mnemonic_to_entropy operation takes a 'mnemonic' field and emits a single line: entropy_hex=<lowercase hex>. For the 24-word phrase starting 'elder lottery unlock common...' the expected output is entropy_hex=475083b81730de275969b1f18db34b7fb4ef79c66aa8efdd7742f1bcfe204097.

The wordlist_contains operation emits one line per word in the input 'words' array, in input order, formatted as <word>=<true|false>. For language=English and words=[abandon, lend, zoo] the output is: abandon=true, lend=true, zoo=true. Each word is checked independently against the specified language's BIP39 wordlist.

trigger_keywords: chain code field name root key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The mnemonic_root_key operation emits two lines: key_hex=<126-character lowercase hex representing the 63-byte extended private key half> and chaincode_hex=<64-character lowercase hex representing the 32-byte chain code>. For the mnemonic starting 'art forum devote street...' the expected key_hex is b8f2bece9bdfe2b0282f5bad705562ac996efb6af96b648f4445ec44f47ad95c10e3d72f26ed075422a36ed8585c745a0e1150bcceba2357d058636991f38a37 and chaincode_hex is 91e248de509c070d812ab2fda57860ac876bc489192c1ef4ce253c197ee219a4.

The path_parse operation emits six lines in order: path=<canonical string>, purpose=<Byron|Shelley|PolicyKeys>, coin=<Ada>, account_index=<int>, role=<ExternalChain|InternalChain|Staking>, index=<int>. Purpose mapping: 44' → Byron, 1852' → Shelley, 1855' → PolicyKeys. Coin 1815' → Ada. Role mapping: 0 → ExternalChain, 1 → InternalChain, 2 → Staking. For path m/44'/1815'/0'/0/0 the output is path=m/44'/1815'/0'/0/0, purpose=Byron, coin=Ada, account_index=0, role=ExternalChain, index=0.

trigger_keywords: what error is emitted for bad derivation path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The path_invalid operation (op=path_invalid) emits error=invalid_path for strings that are neither a complete hardened path nor a valid soft tail. The error token is exactly 'invalid_path' with no additional detail. For input m/1852'/1815' (incomplete — missing account, role, index) the output is: error=invalid_path. No stack trace or runtime exception type must appear in the output.

The blake2b224 operation takes field 'data_hex' (accepted case-insensitively) and emits hash_hex=<56-character lowercase hex>. Input hex is case-insensitive. For input 848AC717B552FCD1F2DCB4933E4A8198187E7E424693B51E1B8B16250F3CADFE the expected output is hash_hex=5cd719e99fdd80fd889e82cf012e64d5da7ab35364bb02163bc93974.

trigger_keywords: extract low bits of byte result field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The last_bits operation takes integer fields 'byte' (0–255) and 'n', and emits result=<int> — the value of the least-significant n bits of the byte. For byte=131 and n=4 the output is result=3. The result field is a plain integer, not hex.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'bech32_decode', 'bech32_is_valid', 'address_parse', 'mnemonic_to_entropy', 'wordlist_contains', 'mnemonic_root_key', 'path_invalid', 'path_parse', 'blake2b224', 'last_bits', 'serialize_tx'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: packaging up transactions into the wire format the network expects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 (Transaction CBOR Serialization): the serialize_tx operation with mode=body that produces a canonical CBOR hex encoding of the transaction body, as shown in public_test_cases/feature8_body_change.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: payment output AND a leftover change output going back to the sender, with the fees and expiry baked in
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The serialize_tx test case in feature8_body_change.json: two outputs (one to payment base address at m/1852'/1815'/0'/0/0 + stake, one to change base address at m/1852'/1815'/0'/1/0 + stake), with fee=143502 and ttl=1000, producing the specific CBOR hex in expected_output.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the key tree thing so you can get keys for any account slot
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4 (BIP32-Ed25519 Hierarchical Key Derivation): mnemonic_root_key, derive_private_key, and derive_public_key operations using BIP32-Ed25519 with hardened (apostrophe-marked) and soft derivation paths as specified in feature4_root_key.json and the path format from feature5_parse.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please check the start.md PRD and the public_test_cases directory for the authoritative specification and expected input/output contracts for that operation.

feature1_decode.json.description: Decode a Bech32-encoded value into its raw data payload, the embedded witness/header version byte, and the human-readable prefix, and report whether the input is well formed. The implementation follows the Bech32 specification used for blockchain address encoding.

feature1_is_valid_false.json.description: A validity predicate reports false for inputs that are not well-formed Bech32 strings, including a null reference, empty or whitespace-only text, strings with no separator or an out-of-range character, and strings carrying a corrupted checksum.

feature2_parse.json.description: Parse a Bech32 address string into a structured address, exposing its raw byte payload as hex, its human-readable prefix, the network it belongs to, and the address type (payment-with-stake base addresses here).

feature3_entropy.json.description: Restore a mnemonic phrase back to the raw entropy it encodes, returned as a hex string. This is the inverse of generating a phrase from entropy under the BIP39 scheme.

input.mnemonic: elder lottery unlock common assume beauty grant curtain various horn spot youth exclude rude boost fence used two spawn toddler soup awake across use

feature3_wordlist_membership.json.description: For each supported language, the corresponding BIP39 wordlist contains a set of known sample words drawn from that language. Membership is checked word by word.

feature4_root_key.json.description: Derive the root (master) extended private key from a restored mnemonic, returning the 64-byte key half and the 32-byte chain code as hex.

input.mnemonic: art forum devote street sure rather head chuckle guard poverty release quote oak craft enemy

feature5_invalid.json.description: Strings that are neither a complete hardened path nor a valid soft tail are rejected when constructing a path. Each such input surfaces as a normalized invalid-path error.

feature5_parse.json.description: Parse a full derivation path string into its components: purpose, coin type, account index, role, and index. Re-rendering the parsed path reproduces the original string.

cases.expected_output:
path=m/44'/1815'/0'/0/0
purpose=Byron
coin=Ada
account_index=0
role=ExternalChain
index=0

feature6_blake2b224.json.description: Compute the 28-byte Blake2b-224 hash of a byte input, returned as hex. This is the key-hash function used to derive credential and key hashes.

feature7_last_bits.json.description: Extract the least-significant N bits of a single byte, returning the resulting integer value.

feature8_body_change.json.description: Serialize an unsigned transaction body that spends one input into a payment output and a change output (both base addresses derived from the same seed), with an explicit time-to-live and fee, to its CBOR encoding (hex).

input.mnemonic: art forum devote street sure rather head chuckle guard poverty release quote oak craft enemy
